coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive carbon dots for the visualization of regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Xiaozhe
Sun, Xiaobo
Chen, Guo
Ding, Laixuan
Li, Yanhua
Liu, Zhike
Wang, Zhijian
Pan, Wei
Hu, Chunhui
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the pH-sensitive carbon dots were synthesized by simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of threonine. The fluorescence of the as-synthesized carbon dots was featured by pH dependence, with the intensity decreasing as pH increases. The carbon dots can be readily internalized into plant pathogenic fungal cells for imaging with dose dependence. Moreover, intracellular pH regulation, a kind of physiological functions of living fungal cells, was visually expressed as the variation of fluorescence intensity of the internalized carbon dots. A transient reduction of the intracellular pH tightly regulated by living fungal cells under basic external condition can be presented as a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. On the contrary, the fluorescence of the carbon dots distributed in apple tissue infected by the fungal cells was soon quenched by the basic buffer because there was no intracellular pH regulation in the dead cells.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Culture and imaging of fungal cells
Characteristics of the fluorescent carbon dots
The optical properties of the as-synthesized carbon dots
The sensitivity and selectivity of the carbon dots fluorescence to pH
The visualization of intracellular pH regulation inside living pathogenic fungal cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Utilization , China

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image of the as-synthesized carbon dots. Insert shows lattice spacing of the carbon dots.
(A) XPS, (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s spectra of the as-synthesized carbon dots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–Vis absorption spectra (Abs) and fluorescence spectra (FLSC, excitation wavelengths as indicated) of the as-synthesized C-dots in aqueous solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 360nm of 0.0036mgmL−1 carbon dots and pH variation in the pH range of 2.13–9.34. Insert: pH variation around physiological condition (6.03–8.91), and the fitted linear curve (F 0 − F)/F 0 =1565.3–35.7pH, R =0.993, F 0 is fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots at pH 2.13. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of plant pathogenic fungal cells (V. mali) incubated in PBS solution containing the as-synthesized carbon dots at different concentrations. (B) Quantitative analysis of the fluorescence intensity of the fungal cells. ∗ p <0.05. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Representative confocal fluorescence images of plant pathogenic fungal cells (V. mali) and the infected apple tissue with time increasing after being incubated in basic PBS solution (pH=10.0). Arrowhead with black core demonstrates living fungal cells. Arrowhead with red cores indicates the infected apple tissue. (B) Quantitative analysis of the fluorescence intensity of the fungal cells and the infected apple tissue in the fluorescence images acquired at intervals of one minute with time increasing after being incubated in PBS solution (pH=10.0). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive carbon dots for the visualization of regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Landscape Architecture and Forestry, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Landscape Architecture and Forestry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the pH-sensitive carbon dots were synthesized by simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of threonine. The fluorescence of the as-synthesized carbon dots was featured by pH dependence, with the intensity decreasing as pH increases. The carbon dots can be readily internalized into plant pathogenic fungal cells for imaging with dose dependence. Moreover, intracellular pH regulation, a kind of physiological functions of living fungal cells, was visually expressed as the variation of fluorescence intensity of the internalized carbon dots. A transient reduction of the intracellular pH tightly regulated by living fungal cells under basic external condition can be presented as a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. On the contrary, the fluorescence of the carbon dots distributed in apple tissue infected by the fungal cells was soon quenched by the basic buffer because there was no intracellular pH regulation in the dead cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials with high performance, have attracted increasing attention due to their fascinating properties, such as high photostability, tunable excitation and emission, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility. These features make carbon dots especially useful for fluorescent bioimaging and serve as a benign yet nontoxic alternative for traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots [1]. Recently, carbon dots originated from various carbon sources and surface passivated by different soft molecules have been demonstrated to be successfully used in the fluorescence imaging of various cells, ranging from bacterial to animal. For example, Escherichia coli cells were stained by the carbon dots passivated by polyethylene glycol for fluorescent imaging [2]. Carbon dots with surface-passivation by 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) were reported for the fluorescence detection of COS-7 cells [3].

To date, there have been remarkable advances in research on the synthesis of carbon dots. Predominant methods for synthesis of carbon dots often include laser ablation [4], electrochemical [5], hydrothermal [6,7], solvothermal [8], microwave [9,10] and ultrasonic [11]. Out of these methods, hydrothermal route is promisingly attractive because it is eco-friendly and can directly lead to carbon dots surface-functionalized by hydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups with good stability and biocompatibility [12]. In addition, protonation and deprotonation of these groups in aqueous solution may attribute to pH-dependent fluorescence property of carbon dots [13]. Therefore, hydrothermal route is facile to synthesize carbon dots besides those that have potentials as colorimetric and fluorescent pH-sensors, with fluorescence emission or intensity being readily tuned by adjusting the pH in aqueous solutions. On the other hand, hydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups on the surfaces of carbon dots have made it easier for their conjugation with active species for specific applications. For instance, 4′-(Aminomethyl phenyl)-2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine (AE-TPY) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were covalently bound onto carbon dots through hydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups on the surfaces to form pH sensors for colorimetrically sensing intracellular pH in living animal cells [14–16].

Intracellular pH plays a critical role in modulating many biological reactions occurring in living cells, which are essential for most cellular processes [17]. Tight regulation of intracellular pH is a significantly important physiological function of living cells [18]. To achieve visualization of such regulation will gain a better understanding of physiological and pathological processes. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on the visualization of intracellular pH regulation inside living cells with carbon dots as probes. In this paper, we applied the facile hydrothermal method to synthesize pH-sensitive carbon dots using threonine as carbon source, without any further modification. The regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal cells was visually expressed as the fluorescence signal variation of the pH-sensitive carbon dots.

Threonine was purchased from Lanji Technology Development Co, LTD (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) was obtained from Sinapharm Chemical Reagent Co. LTD (Shanghai, China). Ninhydrin was purchased from Shanpu Chemical Co. LTD (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. PBS buffer solutions of different pH (2.0–10.0) were prepared by Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, HCl and NaOH. All the other chemicals used were of analytical grade without any further purification.

The carbon dots were synthesized by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment with the typical procedures as follows: 1.3g threonine was dissolved in 26mL ultrapure water. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into the Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclaves, and then placed in a homogeneous reactor followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 5h at a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. After the reaction, the autoclaves were cooled down naturally to room temperature. The resulted brown solution was then centrifuged at 5000rpm for 1h. The supernatants were dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 500) for 3days. Finally, the carbon dots powders were dried under vacuum at ambient temperature for 48h.

The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared carbon dots were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 360nm) and absorbance values at 360nm of the carbon dots aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfate [6,7].

UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, China). Fluorescent measurements were performed with a fluorescence spectrometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan). The morphology and size were analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEOLJEM-2010 electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The carbon dots solutions were carefully dripped onto the 300-mesh copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film, and the solvent was evaporated at ambient temperature and pressure. The results of X-ray diffraction (XRD) were recovered from a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu Kα radiation (60kV, 60mA, λ =1.5406Å) at a scanning speed of 4°/min in the range from 5° to 80°. The FTIR spectrum of the carbon dots was measured on a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer (VG Co., United Kingdom) with AlKα X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. The existence of amino groups on the surfaces of the carbon dots was tested by colorimetric test of ninhydrin reaction. The ImageJ software was used to quantitatively analyze the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culture and imaging of fungal cells
ce:section.ce:para: The fungal cells, Valsa mali Miyabe et Yamada (V. mali), were cultured on potato-dextrose agar (PDA) medium. Fungal discs (each 5mm diameter) were placed in the center of sterile petri dishes containing PDA supplemented with streptomycin at 200μg/mL to reduce bacterial contamination. Then the plates were incubated at 27±2°C for 3days.

An apple was placed in a petri dish, with two lays of filter paper containing appropriate amounts of sterile water to maintain the required humidity. The ager discs of fungal cells (V. mali) for 5mm diameter were put on the apple with mycelia facing the skin surface. The dishes were kept in a constant temperature incubator at 27±2°C for 3days. The slices of infected apple tissue with mycelia (isolated fungal cells) were then cultured in the PBS buffer containing the as-synthesized carbon dots at a certain concentration for 24h. After washed 3 times with PBS buffer, the slice of the infected apple tissue with mycelia (isolated fungal cells) was imaged using a laser scanning confocal microscopy (Lieca, Germany). Whereafter, the dynamic imaging in succession at intervals of one minute was pursued via the laser scanning confocal microscopy when the basic buffer (pH 10.0) was added on the slide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the fluorescent carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM imaging reveals that the as-synthesized carbon dots are mono dispersed nanocrystals of near spherical morphology with an average diameter of 8.2nm (Fig. 1 ). The carbon dots have lattice space of 0.23nm (see the inset of Fig. 1), which is consistent with that of (100) facet of graphite. The XRD pattern measured for the as-synthesized carbon dots shows a broad peak centered at 2θ =23° (d =0.39nm) (see Fig. S1), corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon based materials [13]. FTIR spectra were used to identify the surface functional groups of the as-synthesized carbon dots. As demonstrated in Fig. S2, the broad bands centered at 3408, 3372, and 3172cm−1 suggest the existence of OH, NH2, and NH3 +. The peaks at 1615, 1524, 1404 and 1132cm−1 are assigned to CO, NH, CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. Bands of 2965 and 2864cm−1 are assigned to a CH2 vibration [19]. XPS was performed to further characterize the surface composition and element analysis for the overall composition of the carbon dots (see Fig. 2 ). The XPS spectrum of the carbon dots (Fig. 2A) shows three peaks at 284.0, 400.0, and 531.0eV which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The XPS results indicate that these carbon dots are mainly composed of C (54.4%), N (12.6%), and O (33.0%). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) shows four peaks at 284.4, 285.4, 286.7, and 287.8eV, which are attributed to CC, COH/COC, CN, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) shows three peaks at 399.7, 400.2 and 401.0eV, which are attributed to the CNC, N(C)3 and NH bands. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibits two peaks at 530.5 and 532.1eV, which are attributed to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively. The existence of NH2 on the surfaces of the carbon dots was further confirmed by colorimetric test, in which the carbon dots solution became bluish violet after reacting with ninhydrin under heating in boiling water bath (Fig. S3). By means of the pyrolysis of threonine, the carboxylic, amino and amide groups formed on the carbon dots. The presence of these groups imparted excellent solubility in water without any further chemical modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of the as-synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Measurements of the absorption spectra showed that the as-synthesized carbon dots featured a peak at around 272nm (Fig. 3 ) which is attributed to n–π∗ transition of CO bond [20]. As shown in Fig. 3, the as-synthesized carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviour, also similar to what were reported for carbon dots from other methods and sources [21]. As they were excited at wavelengths from 320 to 440nm, the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelengths. The optimal emission peaks at 388nm when excited at 320nm and then the fluorescence intensity decreased remarkably (Fig. S4). Their fluorescence intensity did not change under continuous illumination with a Xe lamp for 3h (Fig. S5), which suggests that the as-synthesized carbon dots have potential applications for biological labelling and imaging. Using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54.6%) as a reference [20,22], the fluorescent quantum yields of the as-synthesized carbon dots was measured to be 16.33%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity and selectivity of the carbon dots fluorescence to pH
ce:section.ce:para: It was observed that the fluorescence of the carbon dots was sensitive to pH in a broad range of 2.13–9.34, which was featured by a decrease of the fluorescence intensity as the pH increased (see Fig. 4 ). Fluorescence intensity were plotted as a function of pH, with a well fitted sigmoidal curve using Boltzmann equation as shown in Fig. 4. Under around physiological condition (pH 6.03–8.91), the fluorescent emission intensity showed good linearity with pH variation (see insert of Fig. 4). Thus the as-synthesized carbon dots can be explored as a potential pH nanosensor for biological systems. By fitting the fluorescence intensities with the Henderson–Hasselbach equation, a pKa of 8.15±0.06 and a slope of 4.11±0.12 were observed. In addition, since the slope is higher than unity, it suggests that the carbon dots follow a polyelectrolyte ionization [21,23].

The selectivity of the carbon dots fluorescence to pH in complex intracellular environment was estimated, with detecting the interferences of cations, anions, and biomolecules, as well as ionic strength. As shown in Fig. S6, the effects of 11 kinds of cations, including Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, and Fe2+ on the fluorescence response of the carbon dots were explored. Most cations had no effects on the fluorescence emission, except that Fe2+ may have quenched slightly the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots. On the other hand, the interferences of anions (F−, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, Br− and Ac−) were also examined, no obvious signal changes were observed in the aqueous solutions of carbon dots containing different anions compared with the blank (Fig. S7). In addition, negligible responses were exhibited for the carbon dots towards amino acids, glucose, H2O2 and protein (Fig. S8). Moreover, the effects of different ionic strengths (0.01, 0.05, 0.15, and 0.20M KCl) on the fluorescence response of the carbon dots were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S9, the change of ionic strength had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. Therefore, the carbon dots showed the excellent selectivity towards pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visualization of intracellular pH regulation inside living pathogenic fungal cells
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of the pH-sensitive carbon dots as label agents in application for fluorescence imaging of the pathogenic fungal cells (V. mali) was firstly investigated using a confocal microscopy. The isolated fungal cells were cultured in the PBS buffer containing the as-synthesized carbon dots at different concentrations. Upon incubation at the room temperature for 24h, it was observed that the carbon dots were readily internalized into the fungal cells for imaging since they became brightly illuminated when imaged on the confocal microscopy with excitation by 488nm laser pulses (see Fig. 5 ). Analysis of the fluorescent signal at the different doses clearly indicated a dose dependent increase in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5A and B). Quantitative analyses of the fluorescent signal intensity were shown in Fig. 5B. One-way ANOVA followed by least-significant difference (LSD)’s multiple comparison test indicated that only the fungal cells cultured in the PBS solution containing the carbon dots at the concentration of 0.378mg/mL yielded a statistically significant fluorescent signal compared to those in PBS or lower doses of the carbon dots. The corresponding DIC images were shown in Fig. S10.

In addition, photostability of the as-synthesized carbon dots inside living fungal cells was evaluated under continuous illumination on the confocal microscopy with excitation by 488nm laser pulses. As shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity did not change within 100min, suggesting that the as-synthesized carbon dots have excellent photostability and potential for long-time duration dynamic imaging of living fungal cells.

Due to the selectivity and sensitivity to pH in complex intracellular environment, as well as the intracellular photostability, the as-synthesized carbon dots may have the capability of selectively sensing pH change inside living fungal cells, which is an inherent physiological activity i.e. intracellular pH regulation. Therefore, the possibility of the as-synthesized carbon dots as label agents in application for visualization of intracellular pH regulation inside living cells was subsequently explored. A slice of apple tissue infected by fungal cells (V. mali) was labelled by the carbon dots at concentration of 0.378mg/mL, and then cultured in basic buffer (pH=10.0) on a glass slide. Herein, the dead cells of the infected apple tissue were as the contrast to the living cells of plant pathogenic fungi. The role of the basic buffer was to induce the regulation of intracellular pH inside living cells. Dynamic imaging in succession at intervals of one minute was pursued using a confocal microscopy, which was finally presented as a video (video1). The representative images and the quantitative analysis of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots internalized in the fungal cells and the infected apple tissue were shown in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. The strongly basic external condition (pH=10.0) resulted in obvious variations of fluorescence brightness of the carbon dots internalized both in the fungal cells and the infected apple tissue over time (see Fig. 6A, video1). An interesting phenomenon was observed that fluorescence brightness of the pH sensitive carbon dots internalized in fungal cells increased firstly (Fig. 6A, and video1) and reached the highest at 21min as shown in Fig. 6B. Considering the high sensitivity and selectivity of the carbon dots to pH in complex intracellular environment and the photostability in living fungal cells, the reason why the phenomenon occurred was most likely that the basic external condition induced a regulation of intracellular pH resulting in transient reduction of pH inside the living fungal cells, which was achieved by a complex interaction of H+ transport, H+-consuming and -producing reactions, and H+ buffering [22,24]. As a result, the intracellular pH reduction caused the fungal cells to become brighter because the fluorescence of the carbon dots internalized is pH-dependent, with an increase of the fluorescence intensity as pH decreases. Consequently, the regulation of intracellular pH inside living fungal cells was correspondingly exhibited as the variation of fluorescence brightness of the internalized pH-sensitive carbon dots. However, for a longer incubation time (more than 21min) under strongly basic condition, the fungal cells would be damaged, which resulted in the intracellular pH regulation breaking down. Thus, the fluorescence of carbon dots internalized in fungal cells was soon quenched by the basic buffer solution within 5min (see Fig. 6).

Moreover, the same phenomenon was reproduced in isolated fungus labelled by the carbon dots under basic external condition (see video2). As a consequence, the visual expression of intracellular pH regulation as the variation of the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots was further identified.

On the contrary, fluorescence brightness of the pH sensitive carbon dots internalized in the infected apple tissue exhibited significant reduction with time increasing as shown in Fig. 6A, and was nearly totally quenched when incubating in basic buffer solution for more than 15min (see Fig. 6B). It is possible that there was neither intracellular pH regulation nor selective membrane permeability for the dead cells in the infected apple tissue. Thus, basic buffer could be introduced readily into the infected apple tissue, and hence quenched the fluorescence of the internalized carbon dots.

We have employed a one-pot hydrothermal method to prepare carbon dots using threonine as carbon source. The as-synthesized carbon dots have showed strong and stable fluorescence which is dependent on excitation wavelength. Their fluorescence is sensitive to pH in a broad range. Its sensitivity was marked by a decrease of the fluorescence intensity as pH increases. Consequently, intracellular pH regulation, a kind of physiological functions of living cells, could be visually expressed as the fluorescence signal variation of the as-synthesized carbon dots.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by the promotion funds for Outstanding Young Investigators of Shandong Province, China (BS2011NY008), Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Utilization, China (RERU2013005) and The Project-sponsored by SRF, China for ROCS, SEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.09.071.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document file contains Supplementary Figs. S1–S11.
